Search course reserves in Primo: type the textbook title, a course abbreviation (e.g. ECO 201), or your professor's last name [1]. Loan periods for reserve items are chosen by the instructor -- 2-hour checkout for use in the library, 1-day, or 3-day -- and all reserve materials are removed at the end of every semester [1]. The reserves guide has the full details, including how instructors place materials on reserve [1].

To find articles or browse research databases, use the library's Databases A-Z list. It's the authoritative index of every database the library subscribes to -- organized alphabetically and by subject -- and links directly into each database with proper authentication.

Why send you there instead of looking up a specific database name? Database names overlap and shift ("Web of Science" vs "Web of Knowledge", "PsycINFO" vs "Psychology and Behavioral Sciences Collection"); the A-Z page disambiguates them with current names + subject tags.

Databases A-Z: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/az/databases

To find articles or browse research databases, use the library's Databases A-Z list. It's the authoritative index of every database the library subscribes to -- organized alphabetically and by subject -- and links directly into each database with proper authentication.

King Library’s quiet/silent study floor is on the THIRD FLOOR. [1]

Yes -- King Library has lockers in the Reading Rooms. They are restricted to active faculty and actively enrolled graduate students. Locker assignments are requested via an online form on the Reading Rooms page and are assigned yearly on a first-come, first-served basis (with a waitlist when full) [1].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use the Miami University Libraries Printing & WiFi page for current instructions on printing from your laptop [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for the current WiFi connection information [1].
Yes, color printing is available at Oxford libraries. For current locations, instructions, and costs, use the Printing & WiFi page [1].
You can scan a document at the multipurpose printers. See the Printing and WiFi webpage for details [1].
Yes—you can borrow a Chromebook laptop for 30 days from the Oxford libraries. [1]
Chromebook laptops may be checked out for 30 days. [1]
Yes—Oxford Libraries lend chargers for Mac, PC, and phones, along with other peripherals. Check the Tech Equipment for Checkout page for details and availability [1].
The Software on Library Computers page lists the software available on Oxford library computers; ask at the library information desk for details [1].
Yes. MATLAB can be added to library computers using Software Center on campus Windows PCs or Self-Service on campus Macs [1].
Yes. You can add SPSS to Oxford library computers using Software Center on campus Windows PCs or Self-Service on campus Macs [1].
Use the Miami University Libraries APA citation resources page for instructions on citing a website [1].
You can find recommended APA citation generators and tools on the Miami University Libraries citation-resources page [1].
Yes—Miami University Libraries provides Zotero help through citation tutorials, including Zotero and EndNote resources [1].
Yes -- librarians offer research consultations. Find the subject librarian for your course, major, or topic on the subject librarians page and contact them directly to set up an appointment [1].

For help developing a research strategy for your paper, use the Ask Us service to connect with the library, including consultation booking options [1]. You can also identify and contact a subject librarian through the Liaisons page for support in your area of study [2].

Get research help through the Ask Us page, which offers chat, email, appointment, and phone options [1]. For help developing a research strategy, you can also schedule a consultation with a subject librarian [1][2].
Yes. Oxford Libraries’ Data Services page describes support including consultations, GIS help, and data-visualization support [1].
Yes—Oxford Library data services offers consultations, including GIS help and data-visualization support [1]. Library staff are happy to help with GIS projects [2].
Miami University Libraries’ Data Services can help with data analysis, including consultations for R, Python, and statistical analysis [1].

Yes. Miami University librarians provide in-class instruction, workshops, and individual consultations in information literacy, research skills, and library resources [1]. To schedule a session, fill out the Library Instruction Request Form on the instruction page [1].

To schedule a library instruction session, fill out the Library Instruction Request Form on the Library Instruction page. You may also contact the Subject Specialist Librarian in your area of study. [1]
For the Dean of University Libraries and the library administration/leadership team, see the Dean's Office page [1].
The Libraries' staff directory is on the staff page -- you can look up any staff member and their contact information there [1].
Alea Henle is the head of the Access & Borrow department, which encompasses circulation [1]. Her email is henlear@miamioh.edu [2].
King Library is Closed this Saturday (2026-07-18). [1]

The library's hours page always shows the current and upcoming schedule, so please check the date you need there: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/about/locations/hours/.

Library hours vary by building and by term, so none of the libraries are routinely open 24 hours. King Library is the only building that runs near-24-hour schedules, and only during finals periods. Please check the hours page for the building and date you need [1].
Yes. Place a recall or hold through your library account in OhioLINK/Primo; the circulation policies page provides the related borrowing policies. [1][2]
For lost-item fees and the replacement procedure, consult the circulation policies page and contact the circulation desk at the library where you borrowed the book. [1][2]

Miami University Libraries does not issue an alumni library card. For the borrowing options currently available after graduation, please check the circulation policies page [1], or ask the circulation desk at (513) 529-4141.
The library typically holds a book for 7 days after it is ready for pickup. Check the circulation policies for the current hold-shelf period. [1]
You can return books after hours in the exterior book-return drop boxes at the main entrances of King Library. [1][2]
Yes -- scanners is available at all three campuses: Oxford (King), Hamilton (Rentschler), and Middletown (Gardner-Harvey). [1][2][3]
Yes. Rentschler Library on the Hamilton campus offers “Course Reserves and Textbooks on Reserve.” [1]

I'm focused on Miami University Libraries questions -- hours, services, spaces, research help, and policies. The question you asked is outside that scope, so I don't have a reliable answer for it.

If this IS a library question I'm misreading, try rephrasing more specifically. For anything else (campus logistics, course questions, news, etc.), the librarians on Ask Us can usually point you to the right office:

Ask Us: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/research/research-support/ask/

King Library on the Oxford campus has a quiet/silent study floor on the third floor. It also offers study-room reservations, while Wertz Art & Architecture Library has individual and group-study seating and reservable study rooms. [1][2][3]
Yes—Wertz offers color and black-and-white printing for prints up to 11×17 [1]. For current printing instructions and costs, see the Printing & WiFi page [2].
Use a library scanner or multifunction printer and follow the scan-to-email workflow described on the Printing & WiFi page [1].

King Library houses the Walter Havighurst Special Collections [1]. For current instructions on viewing microfilm at King, contact or visit King Library; its location page lists the library’s contact information [1].

Check the King Library LibCal page for current group-study-room availability and rooms that accommodate six people [1]. Study rooms can be reserved up to two weeks in advance, with reservations limited to two hours per day [2].
Yes. Headphones are available for checkout at the circulation desk with your Miami ID; the Libraries’ technology-checkout page provides current equipment details and availability information. [1][2]

After-hours access at King Library depends on the term schedule: it is open 24 hours during finals, and otherwise closes at its posted hours [1][2]. Check the Library Hours page for the current schedule [2].
Please check the King Library location page for current information about nursing or lactation spaces [1].
Please check the floor signage or ask at the King Library service desk for restroom locations [1].
King Library’s location page is the available source for floor descriptions and building information; it also lists the library’s address as 151 S. Campus Ave, Oxford, OH 45056 [1].
King Library provides study rooms, computers, and other equipment [1]. For current information about laptop-charging outlets, contact the library at 513-529-4141 [1].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use the Miami University Libraries Citation LibGuide for guidance on citing a website in MLA format [1].

Find Chicago Manual of Style help in Miami University Libraries’ citation LibGuide [1]. For subject-specific assistance, the guide recommends contacting a subject librarian who specializes in history or humanities [1].

Most article databases (EBSCO, JSTOR, and others) have a 'peer-reviewed' or 'scholarly journals' checkbox filter -- apply it to limit your results to peer-reviewed articles. Pick a database from the Databases A-Z list [1], and if you're not sure which database fits your topic, your subject librarian can recommend one [2].

Miami University theses and dissertations are available through the Libraries’ Digital Collections, which includes a “Miami University Theses & Dissertation” collection [1]. The `/theses/` library shortcut redirects to the graduate writing guide [2].

Yes -- the Libraries support government information research. "Government Information and Law" is one of the Libraries' subject areas, and its subject liaison is Jenny Presnell (Humanities and Social Science Librarian, King Library), whom you can reach through the subject liaisons directory [1]. The research guides page lists the related subject guides [2].
You can ask the library checkout desk whether an HDMI cable is available; equipment availability can vary, so call (513) 529-4141 in advance. Bring your university ID to borrow equipment. [1]

The Libraries list calculators among the equipment available for checkout. [1] Availability can be unpredictable, so call (513) 529-4141 or stop by a checkout desk to confirm whether a graphing calculator is available. [2]
A DSLR camera can be checked out for 24 hours [1].

The Oxford Libraries offer technology equipment for checkout, but USB drives are not listed among the available equipment shown. Call (513) 529-4141 or ask at a library checkout desk to confirm current availability, since equipment availability can be unpredictable [1].

Yes—the Oxford libraries offer chargers for phones to borrow through tech checkout [1]. Because availability can be unpredictable, call (513) 529-4141 or check the tech-checkout page for current iPhone cable compatibility and availability [1].

I can’t file the lost-and-found report for you. Please report your AirPods to the King Library circulation/service desk, or use Ask Us to connect with a librarian [1][2].

I don't have access to your library account. Please check your account at https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en or contact us at (513) 529-4141.

I can't place materials on course reserves for you -- instructors submit reserves requests themselves through the Libraries' reserves process. The Reserves & Textbooks guide has the instructor instructions for placing materials on reserve [1]. If you need help with a request, contact the circulation desk at (513) 529-4141.

I can't submit ILL requests for you -- requests go through the official form so they're tied to your account. Submit yours at https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/borrow/ill/ (takes a couple of minutes). Pickup is at your campus library.

I don't have access to your library account. Please check your account at https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en or contact us at (513) 529-4141.
3D printing at the King Library MakerSpace is often free of charge, but rates can change -- please check the current pricing on the MakerSpace guide before you print [1].
The listed Adobe guidance does not establish eligibility for part-time staff. Please consult the software page for the current eligibility and access instructions [1].
Special Collections reading-room rules include using pencils only, keeping food and drinks out of the room, and using materials under supervised access. [1]
Miami provides New York Times access for affiliated users. The Libraries' New York Times guide has the current activation steps — see [1].

Download and reuse rights vary by collection and item -- check the rights statement on the item's page in Digital Collections [1]. If the rights are unclear or you need permission (for example for a thesis or publication), contact Special Collections & University Archives at SpecColl@MiamiOH.edu or Archives@MiamiOH.edu [2].
